comments:
Summary:
This paper studies irrelevance and independence relations in graphical models whose local specifications are closed convex sets of probability distributions (Quasi-Bayesian networks). Using Walley’s definitions, the author analyzes two types of extensions: type-1 extensions (convex hull of cross-products of local extreme points) and natural extensions (largest joint credal set compatible with local constraints and declared irrelevance relations). Main contributions include: (1) a proof that d-separation relations correspond to Walley independence in type-1 extensions (Theorem 1), (2) formulation of inference in natural extensions as linear fractional programs and reduction strategies (including Theorem 2 that reduces variables using a partition S/W), (3) discussion of how to encode irrelevance and independence constraints and an illustrative example comparing results from different extension types.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important conceptual and practical problem: how to represent and exploit qualitative irrelevance/independence within Quasi-Bayesian networks.
- Clear positioning in the literature (Walley, Cano et al., etc.) and explicit adoption of Walley’s definitions.
- Provides formal results (Theorems and lemmas) connecting graphical d-separation to type-1 extensions and giving reductions for natural-extension optimization.
- Gives concrete algorithms (linear fractional programming reduction to LP) and a worked example that highlights differences between extension types.
- The paper acknowledges computational difficulties and open problems (combinatorial explosion, lack of efficient algorithms for certain independence constraints), which is honest and useful for future work.

Weaknesses and suggestions:
- Some important technical arguments are presented only as sketches; full, detailed proofs are not included in the main text (some are deferred to appendices or cited as available elsewhere). The paper would be strengthened by providing fuller proofs or clearer pointers to complete proofs.
- The practical computational burden of the natural-extension LP formulations is acknowledged but little concrete guidance is given beyond Theorem 2. The paper would benefit from more discussion of algorithmic heuristics, approximations, or complexity analysis to help practitioners apply the methods to nontrivial networks.
- The treatment of representing Xi irrelevant to its nondescendants (the reverse irrelevance needed to enforce independence) is identified as difficult and no efficient solution is provided. A more detailed exploration of special cases where efficient enforcement is possible would be valuable.
- Experimental evaluation is limited to a single illustrative example; inclusion of additional benchmarks (even small networks) demonstrating scalability, runtime, and numerical behavior would make the empirical claims more persuasive.
- Notation is dense in places; some readers may find the presentation terse. A few more explanatory paragraphs around the main derivations would improve accessibility.

Soundness and originality:
- The arguments as presented appear logically coherent and build appropriately on Walley’s framework and prior work. Theorem 1’s conceptual link between d-separation and Walley independence in type-1 extensions is an important clarification. The linear-fractional-program approach to natural extensions is a natural and useful formalization.
- Originality is moderate: the paper consolidates and extends known ideas (type-1 extensions, Walley’s theory) and contributes new formal links and algorithmic formulations. The results are nontrivial and provide useful insight.

Recommendation:
- This is a solid, theoretically grounded contribution that will be of interest to researchers working on imprecise probabilities, graphical models, and robust Bayesian analysis. Before wider adoption, further work on computational methods and additional empirical validation would be desirable. I recommend acceptance (strong) for publication on arXiv; for a conference or journal version I would ask for expanded proofs, additional experiments, and more discussion of algorithms to mitigate combinatorial issues.

Concrete suggestions for revision:
1) Include fuller proofs (or an explicit appendix with complete proofs) for the main theorems to improve verifiability. 2) Provide complexity analysis of the LP/linear-fractional formulations and propose heuristics or approximate methods to handle larger networks (e.g., bounding techniques, constraint generation, decomposition). 3) Expand the experimental section with several example networks of varying size/topology to show how the methods scale and to compare type-1 vs natural extension results in practice. 4) Clarify notation and add more explanatory text in derivation-heavy sections to improve readability.
